qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.title: Sample file-upload endpoint allows unauthenticated memory-exhaustion DoS
qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.advisory_match_reason: The finding is an Express sample-app upload memory exhaustion issue in `sample/29-file-upload` and `packages/platform-express/multer/interceptors/file.interceptor.ts`, while the advisory is a Fastify-specific HEAD-to-GET middleware bypass fixed by changing request-method matching in `packages/core/middleware/middleware-module.ts`.

target_advisory_judgement.reason: No qualifying finding identifies the advisory's Fastify HEAD-request middleware bypass root cause. The only qualifying finding concerns unbounded multipart buffering and response serialization in the `sample/29-file-upload` Express example, which is a different code path, component, and vulnerability mechanism.
evidence: The advisory describes `@nestjs/platform-fastify` GET middleware being skipped when Fastify auto-forwards `HEAD` requests to matching `GET` handlers.

The upstream fix commit `cbdf737cd6e7cefa52d05ecea2ae4af95c464614` adds a HEAD-as-GET special case in `packages/core/middleware/middleware-module.ts` and a Fastify middleware regression test in `integration/hello-world/e2e/middleware-fastify.spec.ts`.

The only qualifying finding reports a DoS in `sample/29-file-upload/src/app.controller.ts` plus `packages/platform-express/multer/interceptors/file.interceptor.ts`, triggered by oversized multipart uploads and `file.buffer.toString()` expansion.
That finding does not involve Fastify, `HEAD` requests, GET middleware execution, middleware bypass, or the advisory's authorization/integrity-impact mechanism.
